narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on profiles like yours from past students. The model estimated a 44.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for rejection—meaning the university decided not to admit you to protect their pass rates and resources. This prediction was driven primarily by three key factors from your academic record, ranked by their influence on the outcome. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.50 stood out as the strongest positive force, actually pushing the prediction toward success since it's higher than the typical 3.21 average, suggesting your college performance signals real potential to pass. However, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3—below the average of about 5.5—had a notable pull in the opposite direction, indicating relatively weaker performance compared to peers that year, which nudged the model toward expecting failure. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 3, also under the typical 5.7 average, reinforced that concern by contributing further to the risk assessment. While your LSAT score of 31 is below the 36.7 norm and your full-time student status aligns with most applicants, these played smaller roles here. Overall, your solid undergrad grades were a bright spot that almost offset the dips in your school rankings, but not quite enough compared to students who typically combine strong deciles across years with high test scores for reliable bar success. This isn't a judgment on your character or effort—many applicants face similar hurdles—but a data-driven call. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it toward the average, strengthening those later-year grades through additional coursework or explanations in a reapplication, and gathering strong letters highlighting your undergrad strengths. With targeted improvements, you could shift that probability in your favor for the next cycle.
